did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized a deep neural network (DNN) based on the Inception-ResNet-v2 architecture to classify dermoscopic images of skin tumors (malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, and benign keratosis). The DNN was trained on the ISIC and Shinshu datasets and its diagnostic performance was compared against 30 Japanese dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is labeled as a 'CONCISE COMMUNICATION' and presents original experimental results comparing the diagnostic performance of a deep neural network (DNN) against 30 Japanese dermatologists. It includes specific sections for Introduction, Methods (describing datasets, DNN architecture, and human readers), Results (reporting sensitivity, specificity, and statistical comparisons), and Discussion, indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: Dermoscopic diagnostic performance of Japanese dermatologists for skin tumors differs by patient origin: A deep learning convolutional neural network closes the gap
brief_summary: This study compared the dermoscopic diagnostic performance of a deep neural network (DNN) based on Inception-ResNet-v2 against 30 Japanese dermatologists for classifying malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, and benign keratosis. The DNN was trained on 12,254 images from the ISIC archive and 594 images from the Shinshu dataset, while human readers evaluated both the local Shinshu and non-local ISIC test sets. The dermatologists' sensitivity for malignancy prediction was significantly higher for the Shinshu set (0.853) than for the ISIC set (0.608), indicating reduced accuracy for non-local populations. In contrast, the DNN maintained high specificity (0.962 for Shinshu and 1.00 for ISIC) at the dermatologists' mean sensitivity level, significantly outperforming the human readers. The results suggest that the diagnostic performance of dermatologists diminishes for patients of non-local populations, particularly regarding dominant skin type. The study concludes that a DNN may help close this diagnostic gap in clinical settings involving diverse patient origins.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four primary diagnostic classes used in the DNN classifier and human dermatologist evaluation. Included in the 'malignancy' group (MM + BCC).
One of the four primary diagnostic classes used in the DNN classifier and human dermatologist evaluation. Included in the 'malignancy' group (MM + BCC).
One of the four primary diagnostic classes used in the DNN classifier and human dermatologist evaluation. Classified as benign.

One of the four primary diagnostic classes used in the DNN classifier and human dermatologist evaluation. Includes solar lentigo, seborrheic keratosis, and lichen planus-like keratosis. Classified as benign.

all_task_types.target: Four diagnostic categories: malignant melanoma (MM), basal cell carcinoma (BCC), melanocytic nevus (MN), and benign keratosis (BK)
all_task_types.details: The DNN is explicitly described as a 'four-disease (MM/BCC/MN/BK) classifier'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for dermoscopic images. Additionally, for performance evaluation, the four-class outputs were mapped to binary outputs for malignancy prediction (MM + BCC vs. benign), which is a derived classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (ISIC Set)
datasets_used.description: A master set created using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2017 dataset, HAM10000 dataset, and BCN20000 dataset. Composed mainly of images from fair-skinned patients.

use.training: Used for training the DNN (combined with Shinshu training set).
use.validation: Used for validation (specifically the ISIC 2017 training/validation split for HAM10000 and BCN20000 components as per Table 1).
use.testing: Used as the 'ISIC test set' for evaluating DNN and dermatologist performance (50 images extracted from HAM10000 and Shinshu 2008-2009 datasets for the specific test subset, though the broader ISIC archive was the source of training data).
use.other: Used as a benchmark comparison set (ISIC 2017 test set with 600 images) for DNN performance evaluation.

datasets_used.name: Shinshu Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images from Japanese patients seen in the Department of Dermatology at Shinshu University Hospital between January 2008 and December 2017. Composed of images from patients with primarily darker skin.

use.training: Used for training the DNN (combined with ISIC training set).
use.validation: Used for validation (cross-validated training set samples).
use.testing: Used as the 'Shinshu test set' for evaluating DNN and dermatologist performance (50 images extracted from Shinshu 2008-2009).
use.other: Used to tune hyperparameters to maximize mean cross-validated four-disease balanced accuracies.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Part of the ISIC set, a large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions.

use.training: Included in the ISIC training set for DNN training.
use.validation: Included in the ISIC 2017 training/validation set for DNN training.
use.testing: Test images were randomly extracted from HAM10000 for the ISIC test set (50 images total: 12 MM, 12 BCC, 13 MN, 13 BK).

datasets_used.name: BCN20000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Part of the ISIC set, containing dermoscopic lesions in the wild.

use.training: Included in the ISIC training set for DNN training.
use.validation: Included in the ISIC 2017 training/validation set for DNN training.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Challenge Part 3 Test Set
datasets_used.description: A benchmark test set from the ISIC 2017 Challenge used for external performance comparison of the DNN.

use.testing: Used as a benchmark test set to compare DNN performance against previous studies (ISIC 2017 Challenge Part 3).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and differential diagnostic accuracy values for human readers and the DNN.
To compare the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, accuracy, specificity) of dermatologists between the Shinshu and ISIC test sets, and to compare the DNN's performance against human readers.
To determine statistical significance for differences in diagnostic performance between groups.
To tune hyperparameters of the DNN by maximizing mean cross-validated four-disease balanced accuracies (or macro-averaged recalls) on the training set.
To confirm the statistical variability of the DNN’s performance for malignancy prediction using cross-validated training set samples.
To calculate the area under the curve (AUC) for the malignancy prediction ability of the DNN and to benchmark DNN performance against previous studies.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical comparison of proportions (likely McNemar's test or similar for paired binary outcomes, though not explicitly named, inferred from context of comparing sensitivities/specificities between groups and datasets with P-values reported)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, or accuracy) between the compared groups (e.g., dermatologists on Shinshu vs. ISIC sets, or DNN vs. human readers).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in diagnostic performance between the compared groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values less than 0.001 were considered statistically significant. The study found that dermatologists' sensitivity for malignancy prediction was significantly higher for the Shinshu set than for the ISIC set (P < 0.001). The DNN's specificity and malignancy prediction accuracy were significantly higher than those of human readers (both P < 0.001). No significant difference was found in specificity between dermatologists on the two sets (P = 0.745).

Shinshu Test Set.description: Test set containing 50 images extracted from Shinshu 2008-2009 dataset (12 MM, 12 BCC, 13 MN, 13 BK).
Shinshu Test Set.notes: DNN specificity and malignancy prediction accuracy were significantly higher than human readers (P < 0.001). Differential diagnostic accuracies of human readers for MM and BCC were more than 0.3 lower than those of the DNN.

ISIC Test Set.description: Test set containing 50 images extracted from HAM10000 and Shinshu 2008-2009 datasets (12 MM, 12 BCC, 13 MN, 13 BK).
ISIC Test Set.notes: Dermatologists' sensitivity for malignancy prediction was significantly lower than for the Shinshu set (P < 0.001). Specificity did not differ significantly between sets (P = 0.745). DNN specificity and malignancy prediction accuracy were significantly higher than human readers (P < 0.001).

ISIC 2017 Challenge Part 3 Test Set.description: Benchmark test set containing 600 images (117 MM, 0 BCC, 393 MN, 90 BK).

dnn.performance_comparison: Exceeded the top average for the ISIC 2017 Challenge Part 3

ISIC 2017 Challenge Part 3 Test Set.notes: The DNN performance benchmark test showed that the DNN exceeded the top average recorded in the previous study. BCC score was ignored to match the conditions of the challenge.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on the ISIC set (12,254 images from ISIC 2017, HAM10000, and BCN20000 datasets) and the Shinshu set (594 images). Hyperparameters were tuned to maximize mean cross-validated four-disease balanced accuracies (or macro-averaged recalls) on the Shinshu training set and HAM10000 dataset.

architecture_details.preprocessing: Luminance and color balance normalized using the Shades of Gray algorithm. Ad hoc linear approximation was removed compared to previous studies.
architecture_details.task: Four-disease classifier (Malignant Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Benign Keratosis)

training_details.hyperparameters: Tuned to maximize mean cross-validated four-disease balanced accuracies (or macro-averaged recalls).

classification_or_output_head.details: Outputs mapped to binary outputs for malignancy prediction (MM + BCC vs. benign) based on disease attributes.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep neural network (DNN) based on Inception-ResNet-v2 architecture
preprocessing_techniques: Luminance and color balance normalization using the Shades of Gray algorithm
Removal of ad hoc linear approximation
Manual exclusion of lesions that were highly ulcerated and/or bleeding substantially
conclusions: The dermoscopic diagnostic performance of Japanese dermatologists for skin tumors diminished for patients of non-local populations, particularly in relation to the dominant skin type.
The sensitivity for malignancy prediction by dermatologists was significantly higher for the local Shinshu test set (0.853) than for the non-local ISIC test set (0.608).
A deep neural network (DNN) maintained high specificity (0.962 for Shinshu set and 1.00 for ISIC set) at the dermatologists' mean sensitivity level, significantly outperforming human readers.
The DNN's malignancy prediction accuracy (0.940 for both sets) was significantly higher than that of the human readers.
The DNN may help close the diagnostic gap in clinical settings involving diverse patient origins.

The performance of the DNN for the Shinshu test set improved when images from the local Shinshu population were added to the training set, indicating the importance of training DNNs with images from the local population.
The differential diagnostic accuracies of human readers for malignant melanoma and basal cell carcinoma were more than 0.3 lower than those of the DNN for the ISIC test set.
important_remarks: The study has a small test set size (50 images per dataset), which limits the statistical power of the human reader comparisons.
Complete evaluator blindness could not be ensured because the human readers and test images belonged to the same institute (Shinshu University), posing a risk of recall bias.
To mitigate recall bias, the test images were extracted from the earliest available period (Shinshu 2008–2009) to minimize the likelihood of readers having seen the images previously.

The DNN was trained on a master set combining ISIC (fair-skinned predominant) and Shinshu (darker-skinned) datasets, but the ISIC set contained no Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) images in the training split derived from ISIC 2017/HAM10000/BCN20000 components, relying on Shinshu for BCC representation.
The DNN's performance on the ISIC 2017 Challenge Part 3 benchmark ignored the BCC score to match the challenge conditions, which may not reflect real-world diagnostic utility for BCC.

The study highlights a significant population bias in human dermatologists' performance, with sensitivity dropping significantly (0.853 to 0.608) when evaluating non-local (fair-skinned) populations compared to local (darker-skinned) populations.

The DNN maintained high specificity (1.00) on the ISIC test set at the dermatologists' mean sensitivity level, significantly outperforming human readers, suggesting AI may be more robust to population shifts than human experts in this context.
